surface: AI Factories are the Industrial Infrastructure of the AI Era
why_it_matters: Strongest single thesis surface in the deck; converts the chapter from product chronology to source-actor stagecraft.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA framed AI factories as industrial infrastructure, with inference as workload, tokens as commodity, and compute as revenue.
blocked_claims: No neutral industry definition, independent economics, deployed-factory performance, or revenue proof.
link_to_later_work: Matches GTCX-0192-001; choose one of A-0004/A-0024/A-0106.

why_it_matters: Best handoff from stage rhetoric to actual chips, systems, facilities, software, cooling, and power constraints.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA presented DSX as an AI factory platform spanning chips, systems, facilities, software, reference designs, cooling, and power.
blocked_claims: No customer deployment, adoption scale, facility performance, energy efficiency, or proof that DSX solves power/cooling/interconnection.
link_to_later_work: Matches GTCX-0192-002; choose one of A-0009/A-0029/A-0107.

surface: NVIDIA Vera Rubin 7 Chips - 5 Rack Systems
why_it_matters: Best single diagram/table for NVIDIA's one-gigawatt system comparison and rack-scale AI-factory claim.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA compared an X86 plus Hopper one-gigawatt AI factory frame with a Vera Rubin system across GPUs, FLOPS, bandwidth, and tokens per second.
blocked_claims: No exact ratios, token throughput, revenue, deployed capacity, or neutral performance proof without independent corroboration.
link_to_later_work: Matches GTCX-0192-003; prefer A-0027 card over raw slide.

surface: NVIDIA Extreme Co-Design Delivering X-Factors Every Year
why_it_matters: Best cadence visual: Blackwell to Rubin to Feynman and chips-to-racks-to-factories sequencing.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA showed a 2024/2026/2028 platform roadmap spanning GPUs, CPUs, networking, BlueField, NVLink, and rack-scale systems.
blocked_claims: No future roadmap item as happened history, guaranteed delivery, production status, specs, or timing.
link_to_later_work: Matches GTCX-0192-004; use A-0028 only if roadmap cadence is needed.

surface: Inference Performance and Efficiency Drive Company Results
why_it_matters: Most chart-like token-economics surface: throughput per MW, interactivity, service tiers, and model classes in one visual.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA framed inference throughput per megawatt and interactivity as business-relevant AI factory metrics.
blocked_claims: No exact TPS/MW values, company-result proof, cost-per-token, energy-per-token, or model-rank inference without extraction and corroboration.
link_to_later_work: Reserved in GTCX-0192-006 for future chart mining.

surface: NVIDIA Extreme Co-Design Revolutionized Token Cost
why_it_matters: Directly links token cost, interactivity, tokens per watt, and factory revenue; strongest economics chart before the staged page 32 sequence.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA presented a token-cost and tokens-per-watt comparison for GB300 NVL72, H200 NVL8, and competition.
blocked_claims: No lower-cost or perf/watt claim as independent fact; no price-quality, endpoint, workload, or energy-per-token conclusion without same-scope evidence.
link_to_later_work: Newly elevated by I-0184; not in I-0192 core sequence.

surface: NVIDIA Vera Rubin Unlocks $150B Revenue Opportunity
why_it_matters: High drama and high risk: makes NVIDIA's revenue-per-gigawatt argument visible but must be quarantined as promotional.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA connected Vera Rubin to annual revenue per gigawatt and a stated revenue opportunity.
blocked_claims: No independent revenue opportunity, actual revenue, ROI, deployed capacity, customer economics, or neutral valuation.

surface: NVIDIA Vera Rubin plus LPX Unlocks $300B Revenue Opportunity
why_it_matters: Useful sequel showing how LPX changes NVIDIA's internal economics story, but even riskier than page 37.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA extended the annual revenue per gigawatt comparison to a Rubin plus LPX frame.
blocked_claims: No $300B opportunity as fact, no delivered revenue, no customer economics, no AI-factory ROI proof.
link_to_later_work: Newly elevated by all-page scan; reserve behind page 37.

why_it_matters: Most complete later-stage build of the throughput/interactivity chart including Rubin plus LPX.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA framed Rubin plus LPX, Rubin NVL72, Blackwell NVL72, and Hopper in a throughput/interactivity chart.
blocked_claims: No performance-ratio, throughput, interactivity, tier, model-capability, or cost claim without extraction and triangulation.
link_to_later_work: Newly elevated by all-page scan; reserve behind page 32.

surface: Rubin NVL72 / Blackwell NVL72 / Hopper comparison
why_it_matters: Useful generation comparison if the chapter needs hardware cadence without the 2028 roadmap.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA compared Rubin, Blackwell, and Hopper in throughput/interactivity space with performance-ratio annotations.
blocked_claims: No 10X/3X/2X claims as independent fact; no model-rank, cost, or energy proof.

surface: Uniting Processors of Extreme FLOPS and Bandwidth
why_it_matters: Only mined page that teaches an inference mechanism: prefill, decode, KV cache, Dynamo, Vera Rubin, and Groq 3 LPX.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA linked prefill, decode, and KV cache stages to specialized processors in its inference-compute argument.
blocked_claims: No architecture optimality, shipped integration, performance benefit, model behavior, or vendor superiority without corroboration.

why_it_matters: Concise inference-turn slide, useful as setup if page 32/27 are too dense for layout.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA framed inference compute as entering a new scaling phase and referenced ChatGPT compute.
blocked_claims: No verified 10000X claim, product-demand proof, model behavior proof, or neutral compute requirement.

why_it_matters: Maps NVIDIA's platform breadth around inference frameworks, agent protocols, frontier builders, and software-development categories.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA presented an AI platform map spanning inference, agent frameworks, protocols, model builders, and vertical AI categories.
blocked_claims: No ecosystem dominance, adoption, standardization, robotics relevance, or category completeness claim.

surface: NVIDIA AI Platform with Google Cloud AI Hypercomputer
why_it_matters: Shows AI-factory stack pattern in a cloud-provider context, but lower than generic DSX source surface.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA mapped its platform onto Google Cloud infrastructure, data systems, AI platforms, and open software.
blocked_claims: No cloud adoption, cost savings, customer productivity, performance, or platform superiority.
link_to_later_work: Newly elevated by all-page scan; reserve only.

surface: IBM Storage Scale / Nestle operations data case
why_it_matters: One of the few data-operation charts, but it is partner/testimonial evidence rather than core GTC thesis.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA showed an IBM/Nestle data-processing case with time and cost-savings claims.
blocked_claims: No customer productivity, generalized enterprise savings, exact cost/time claims, or quote use without visual verification and source-chain review.
link_to_later_work: Newly elevated by all-page scan; reserve only.

why_it_matters: Useful if networking/context movement needs a concrete GTC source surface.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA announced BlueField-4 STX with tokens/sec, networking bandwidth, and shared-context-per-GPU claims.
blocked_claims: No product availability, performance, customer deployment, or networking superiority without official release corroboration.

why_it_matters: Can connect the GTC chapter to agent chapters, but should not dominate Chapter 15.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA framed agents as a new computing platform.
blocked_claims: No agent autonomy, productivity, adoption, safety, platform dominance, or coding-agent proof.

why_it_matters: Important negative control: chart-like and LLM-relevant, but unsafe for GTC visual program because it invites leaderboard crowns.
allowed_caption: NVIDIA presented Nemotron 3 Ultra benchmark/efficiency claims against GLM and Kimi.
blocked_claims: No best-open-model claim, live rank, reasoning superiority, benchmark transfer, model capability, or vendor comparison without independent benchmark normalization.
